coredata.dc:title: The preparation of carbon dots/ionic liquids-based electrolytes and their applications in quasi-solid-state dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Huayu
Zhang, Xiuhua
Dong, Binghai
Lu, Hongbing
Zhao, Li
Wan, Li
Dai, Guotian
Wang, Shimin
coredata.dc:description: A facile means to generate carbon dots/ionic liquids (ILs) blend using ionic liquid-assisted electrochemical exfoliation was demonstrated. Two kinds of ILs, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([bmim][BF4]) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([bmim][PF6]), were used in this work. Transmission electron microscopy and UV–vis spectrum were employed to characterize the formed carbon dots/ILs. The carbon dots/ILs were used for fabricating quasi-solid-state dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), where 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide and LiI/I2 were added to enhance the performance of DSSCs. Effects of the varied contents of components in the complex on the performance of DCCSs have been studied in detail at ambient temperature. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy showed that the introduction of carbon dots into ionic liquids can enhance the electrical properties by facilitating charge transfer processes of the electrolytes. The overall energy-conversion efficiency (η) was 2.71% and 2.41% for carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and carbon dots/[bmim][BF4] based blend electrolytes, respectively. A 82% enhancement in η was obtained by introduction of carbon dots into [bmim][PF6] comparing with pure [bmim][PF6] (η =1.49%). In addition, the cells exhibited good stability under continuous illumination in room temperature without any further sealing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar cell
Quasi-solid-state electrolytes
Ionic liquid
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes
Preparation of nanoparticulate TiO2 film and cells assembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of carbon dots obtained from (A) [bmim][PF6]; (B) [bmim][BF4] as the electrolyte. Insets: the photographs of (A) carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and (B) carbon dots/[bmim][BF4].
Viscosity plots for carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] (a), carbon dots/[bmim][BF4] (b), [bmim][PF6] (c), and [bmim][BF4] (d) at various temperatures. Inset: the magnitude of curves (b–d).
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) traces of (a) [bmim][PF6], (b) carbon dots/[bmim][PF6], (c) [bmim][BF4], and (d) carbon dots/[bmim][BF4]. For clarity, curves have been shifted vertically.

UV–vis spectra of carbon dots/[bmim][PF6]-based electrolytes varying with volume of LiI/I2: (a) 0, (b) 25, (c) 100, and (d) 200μL. The electrolytes contained 2.4mg carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and 23mg [bmim]I.

Photocurrent–voltage curves of the DSSC with (A) carbon dots/[bmim][PF6]-based electrolytes with various carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] contents; (B) Carbon dots/[bmim][BF4]-based electrolytes with various carbon dots/[bmim][BF4] contents. The electrolytes contained 23mg [bmim]I, 10μmol LiI and 1μmol I2.

Nyquist plots of DSSCs based on different electrolyte compositions (A) (●) carbon dots/[bmim][PF6], (○) [bmim][PF6], (□) [bmim]I; (B) (●) carbon dots/[bmim][BF4], (○) [bmim][BF4]. The insets show equivalent circuit model of the DSSC.
The DSSC performance for carbon dots/[bmim][PF6]-based blend electrolytes with different LiI/I2 content. The electrolytes contained 2.4mg carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and 23mg [bmim]I.
The DSSC performance for carbon dots/[bmim][PF6]-based blend electrolytes with different [bmim]I content. The electrolytes contained 2.4mg carbon dots/[bmim][PF6], 10μmol LiI and 1μmol I2.
The DSSC performance for [bmim][PF6] electrolyte, various carbon dots/ILs content blend electrolytes. The electrolytes contained 23mg [bmim]I, 10μmol LiI and 1μmol I2.
Parameters obtained by fitting the impedance spectra of DSSCs using the equivalent circuit in Fig.4.
Decay test data of DSSC based on carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes measured repeated at various time intervals.

head.ce:title: The preparation of carbon dots/ionic liquids-based electrolytes and their applications in quasi-solid-state dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ► A facile means to generate carbon dots/ionic liquids (ILs) – were demonstrated. ► The carbon dots/ILs blend were used for fabricating quasi-solid-state DSSCs. ► Cells exhibited good stability in room temperature without any further sealing.

A facile means to generate carbon dots/ionic liquids (ILs) blend using ionic liquid-assisted electrochemical exfoliation was demonstrated. Two kinds of ILs, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([bmim][BF4]) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([bmim][PF6]), were used in this work. Transmission electron microscopy and UV–vis spectrum were employed to characterize the formed carbon dots/ILs. The carbon dots/ILs were used for fabricating quasi-solid-state dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), where 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide and LiI/I2 were added to enhance the performance of DSSCs. Effects of the varied contents of components in the complex on the performance of DCCSs have been studied in detail at ambient temperature. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy showed that the introduction of carbon dots into ionic liquids can enhance the electrical properties by facilitating charge transfer processes of the electrolytes. The overall energy-conversion efficiency (η) was 2.71% and 2.41% for carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and carbon dots/[bmim][BF4] based blend electrolytes, respectively. A 82% enhancement in η was obtained by introduction of carbon dots into [bmim][PF6] comparing with pure [bmim][PF6] (η =1.49%). In addition, the cells exhibited good stability under continuous illumination in room temperature without any further sealing.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) has attracted much attention since the first work reported by Grätzel in 1991 [1]. More recently, a photo-to-current conversion efficiency of more than 12.3% has been achieved in a liquid-electrolyte-based DSSC [2]. The use of liquid electrolytes, however, demands perfect sealing of the device to avoid leakage and evaporation of the solvents. The issues affect the long-time stability and performance of the liquid electrolyte. Thus, much work has been done to replace the liquid electrolytes with solid-state organic hole-transport materials [3], p-type inorganic semiconductors (CuI, CuSCN) [4], polymer electrolytes [5], gel electrolytes [6] and ionic liquids (ILs). However, compared with the liquid electrolytes, DSSCs with quasi-solid- or solid-state electrolytes have relatively low conversion efficiencies mainly owing to their low conductivity or the poor interfacial contact between the dye-sensitized TiO2 film and the electrolytes [7].

Recently, room-temperature ionic liquids (RTILs) such as imidazolium iodide/triiodide have been widely used in DSSCs as a solvent, because of their favorable properties such as negligible vapor pressure, high ionic conductivity, thermal stability, and a possible wide electrochemical window [8]. Usually, binary ionic liquids or combination of RTILs with other materials were used as electrolytes for DSSCs. Binary ionic liquids, usually composed of an imidazolium iodide and another ionic liquid with relatively low viscosity, exhibited high power-conversion efficiencies [9]. These DSSC devices also posed practical limitations to sealing. In order to reduce the fluidity of RTIL-based electrolytes, combination of RTILs with small-molecular gelator [10], polymer [11] and nanoparticles [7,12,13] can produce quasi-solid-state electrolytes. Lei et al. [12] have obtained all-solid-state electrolytes by simply mixing 1-methyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide ([mpim]I) and carbon black. Chen et al. [7] have prepared stable gel electrolytes by mixing fumed silica nanoparticles (5wt%) with [mpim]I/1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([bmim][PF6]) and [mpim]I/[bmim][PF6]/N-methylbenzimidazole by stirring and subsequently sonicating. Such nanocomposite gels can not only solidify the electrolytes, but also accommodate appropriate solvents within the liquid subphase so that ionic conductivity can be raised to a satisfactory degree [14]. These reported solid-state electrolytes were prepared by simply mixing, stirring or sonicating nanoparticles within ILs, which were hard to be homogenized, especially nanoparticles possessing such high surface energy. In this work, one-pot synthesis of nanoparticles in RTILs under mild conditions was applied.

Most recently, the advantages of RTILs in inorganic synthesis have been gradually realized [15]. Various nanoparticles including carbon nanomaterials have been formed in RTILs. The high dielectric constants of ionic liquids (ILs) provided shielding for the stacking interaction and help to disperse nanomaterials effectively. Recently, Liu et al. [16] have demonstrated one electrochemical approach for the preparation of ionic liquid-functionalized graphite sheets by exfoliating graphite in ionic liquid/water. The main purpose of their work was to prepare graphite sheets. After that, Lu et al. [17] demonstrated the similar means to generate carbon nanoribbons, nanoparticles, and graphene from graphite electrode using RTIL-assisted electrochemical exfoliation. And they have developed strategies, such as changing exfoliated time and the water content, to control the distribution of the exfoliated products. The resulting carbon nanomaterials/ILs showed good miscibility. Moreover, the merits of simple preparation and easy control of nano-particles content within the compositional regime prompted us to investigate them as a host for quasi-solid-state electrolytes in DSSC.

In this paper, we reported the preparation and characterization of carbon dots/ILs. The applications of the carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes in DSSCs were also performed. The electrolytes were prepared by commixing carbon dots/ILs with [bmim]I, LiI and I2. The cells presented in the present work were easy to assemble. The electrolytes were coated on the dye-soaked TiO2 photoanode electrode using a thin brush. Moreover, the cells were neither sealed nor encapsulated, and assembled under ambient conditions.

The commercial TiO2 nanoparticles (P25) were purchased from Degussa and used as received. Graphite rod was obtained from Shanghai carbon factory. Cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II) bis-tetrabutylammonium (N719) was purchased from Dyesol. The room temperature ionic liquids, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([bmim][BF4]) and [bmim][PF6], were purchased from Chengjie chemical Ltd. Corp. (Shanghai, China). 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([bmim]I) was purchased from J&K. Acetonitrile was purchased from Alfa Aesar. LiI, I2 and tert-butylpyridine were from Aldrich. The reagents were used as purchased without any further pretreatment. FTO (Hartford Glass Co., Newington, CT; sheet resistance 14–20Ω/cm2) were used as substrates and pretreated by rinsing in an ultrasonic bath of detergent and acetone for 10 and 20min in turn, then rinsing with a large amount of deionized water and ethanol, and at last drying at 80°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots/ILs were prepared by the electrochemical functionalization of graphite rods based on a modified literature method [17]. Briefly, 2mL [bmim][BF4]/water (60:40) or [bmim][PF6]/water (60:40) was used as the electrolyte. Two high-purity graphite rods placed parallel were inserted as electrode into the electrolyte. A static potential of 10V was applied between the two graphite rods. After 30min, the anode graphite rod was corroded, and then a black precipitate gradually appeared at the bottom of the reactor. The electrolyte was taken out of the reactor after corrosion at room temperature for 3h to form a homogeneous solution. The products were washed with ethanol for several times and ultracentrifuged at 15,000rmp at room temperature, and then dried in an oven at 80°C. Finally, the product, namely, carbon dots/IL was obtained.

In order to obtain the optimal conversion efficiency, 0.1M LiI and 0.01M I2 dissolved in acetonitrile (LiI/I2 =10 (molar ratio)), and [bmim]I were added into the above product under continuous stirring. The blend electrolytes were then heated (80°C) to evaporate the solvent. The prepared electrolytes were carbon dots/[bmim][PF6]-based blend electrolytes or carbon dots/[bmim][BF4]-based blend electrolytes (here denoted collectively as carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes). For comparison, [bmim][PF6] and [bmim][BF4] electrolytes were also prepared without the addition of carbon dots while keeping the other preparation conditions unchanged (here denoted collectively as ILs electrolytes). [bmim]I electrolyte was prepared by mixing [bmim]I and LiI/I2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanoparticulate TiO2 film and cells assembly
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare TiO2 paste, ethyl cellulose and terpineol were added into the ethanol solution of the TiO2 powder, and then ethanol was removed from the solution using a rotary evaporator to obtain a viscous paste [18]. The obtained TiO2 paste was deposited using doctor blade onto the FTO glass and sintered for 30min at 450°C in oven (heating rate: 10°Cmin−1). The thickness of this layer was controlled by the adhesive tape. When cooling to 80°C, the TiO2 photoanodes were immersed into a 0.3mM solution of N719 in anhydrous ethanol and kept at room temperature for 24h to complete the sensitizer uptake. A Pt counter electrode was prepared by spin coating H2PtCl6 solution (Aldrich, 0.05moldm−3 in isopropyl alcohol) onto the FTO conductive glass and then sintering it at 400°C for 30min.

A quasi-solid-state dye-sensitized solar cell was assembled in a typical sandwich-type cell. The TiO2 photoanode was coated with tert-butylpyridine. After several minutes, the electrolyte was coated on the dye-soaked electrode using a thin brush. The platinum coated FTO glass plate was placed over the electrolyte and the assembly was left for drying without any further sealing.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed with a JEOLTEM-2010F (JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV, was used to observe or determine the morphology, size, and identity of nanocrystal particles. UV–vis spectra were recorded using an UV–visible spectrometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan) with a wavelength range from 300 to 700nm. The viscosity was measured by SNB-2 digital rotational viscometer (Shanghai Geology Instrument Institute, China) using a small sample adapter assembly. The viscometer was placed in a thermostatic water bath (SD-2001, Shanghai Geology Instrument Institute, China) whose temperature was regulated to within ±0.1°C. For each IL, the experimental viscosity was obtained by averaging three to five flow time measurements. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was carried out on a Shimadzu DSC-60A. 5mg samples were taken at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 in a temperature range of 25–250°C under N2 atmosphere. The solar light was produced by a solar simulator (Newport 91160) at 100mWcm−2 (1 sun) intensity. Electrochemical measurements were performed using Zahner IM6 electrochemical workstation (Zahner-elektrik GmbH & Co. KG). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements of all the DSSCs were recorded under 100mWcm−2 light illumination condition. The frequency range was explored from 0.5mHz to 100kHz, and the applied bias voltage and ac amplitude were set at open-circuit voltage of the DSSCs and 10mV, respectively. The active area of DSSCs was 5mm×5mm (0.25cm2).

Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of carbon dots obtained from [bmim][PF6] and [bmim][BF4] as the electrolytes. TEM images revealed that the nanoparticles were relatively well dispersed and the primary nanoparticles could be readily distinguished. But the images exhibited some degree of agglomeration. Higher extent of agglomeration occurred when [bmim][BF4] was used as the electrolyte (Fig. 1b). It could be seen that the primary particle size was about 5–10nm. The insets of Fig. 1 show the photographs of the carbon dots/ILs.

It is well established that viscosity is one of the most important properties of ILs and sensitive to the presence of additives. The high viscosities of ILs are a direct consequence of their high molecular weights [19]. The viscosities of carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and carbon dots/[bmim][BF4] at 30°C were 3652 and 366cP, respectively. The values were remarkably higher than those of pure [bmim][PF6] and [bmim][BF4], which suggested that the addition of massive carbon dots could remarkably increase the viscosity of ILs. Fig. 2 shows the relationships of viscosity and temperature from 30°C up to 80°C for carbon dots/ILs mixtures. The viscosities of pure ILs were also listed for comparison. The viscosity decreased with the increasing temperature for all the samples, and the values are very close at higher temperatures. Since viscosity is mainly dependent on intermolecular interactions (H-bonding, dispersive and coulombic interactions), an increase in temperature will substantially decrease the intensity of H-bonding interactions and therefore the viscosity [19].

To further investigate the thermal properties of carbon dots/ILs, DSC scans were performed (Fig. 3 ). No peak was observed below 200°C for pure ILs (curves a and c), suggesting that no phase transition of [bmim][PF6] or [bmim][BF4] happened, which were consistent with the previous reports [7,20]. DSC thermogram of carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and carbon dots/[bmim][BF4] showed an endothermic peak centered around 97 and 135°C, respectively (curves b and d). This endothermic peak revealed that crystalline regions were formed in the composite, and that the composite remained in the gel state below the temperatures [7,20].

One of the conditions for the electrolyte to be suitable for the application in DSSCs was that the light absorption by the electrolyte in visible region should be low, which was to ensure the maximum absorption by sensitizer. UV–vis spectra of electrolytes with different LiI/I2 concentration were measured (Fig. 4 ). The peak around 365nm indicated the presence of I3 −. The intensity and width of the absorption peak increased with the increase of LiI/I2 concentration in the electrolyte. According to Ref. [21], the increase in concentration of LiI/I2 acted in favor of I3 − formation, which was necessary for the cell functioning at the beginning. However, further increase in the concentration of LiI/I2 resulted in that I3 − obtained electrons from TiO2 photoanode. Furthermore, I2 also adsorbed some visible lights within the wavelength range from 400 to 500nm, lowering the visible light absorption by the sensitizer [22]. A comparatively low absorption of carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] in the visible region suggested that carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] was suitable for use as electrolyte in DSSCs.

Photocurrent voltage characteristics of the DSSCs using carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes with various LiI/I2 contents were measured under 100mWcm−2 illumination. The dependence of open-circuit photovoltage (V OC), short-circuit photocurrent density (J SC), fill factor (FF), and conversion efficiency (η) on the LiI/I2 concentrations were shown in Table 1 . It was observed that V OC, J SC and FF increased when the contents of LiI/I2 increased from 2.5/0.25 to 10/1μmol. Further increasing LiI/I2 led to the decrease of V OC, J SC and FF. The poor FF (<0.5) at low LiI/I2 contents was due to the depletion of the I3 − at the Pt cathode [23] or the low conductivity of the electrolyte [24]. The optimal content of LiI/I2 was found to be 10/1μmol.

The quasi-solid-state DSSCs with a composite electrolyte consisting of various [bmim]I contents were also measured, where [bmim]I functioned as a carrier mediator between sensitized TiO2 and electrolyte [25]. The solar cell performances, including J SC, V OC, FF, and η, which were obtained from the photocurrent density–voltage (I–V) curves, were summarized in Table 2 . DSSCs employing the electrolytes with [bmim]I exhibited a higher energy conversion efficiency. One can observed that η increased with the increase of [bmim]I contents, and the highest η was found to be 2.71% with a [bmim]I content of 23mg. However, further increase of [bmim]I content led to the reduction of η. The initial increase in η was due to the increased number of charge carriers, and the subsequent decrease was resulted from the formation of ion pairs or higher-order ionic aggregates in the electrolytes [26].

Fig. 5A and B displays the I–V curves of the DSSCs with the varied contents of carbon dots/ILs in the complex as the quasi-solid-state electrolyte. The DSSCs performances were shown in Table 3 . The value of V OC decreased with subsequent increase of carbon dots/ILs. However, value of J SC was found to increase markedly for the optimal content. The gradual increase in J SC maybe attributed to the increase of the electron exchange efficiency of carbon dots/ILs electrolyte in DSSCs. However, the subsequent increase of carbon dots/ILs did not help to increase the value of conversion efficiency further. Comparing with carbon dots/IL-based electrolyte, DSSC with [bmim][PF6] electrolyte exhibited lower J SC and η. The presence of carbon dots might result in the good contact between the electrode and the electrolyte or the increase of conductivity. More detailed explanations for the enhancement of η for carbon dots/IL-based electrolyte will be further discussed later.

In order to confirm the speculation that carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes were beneficial to expedite the electron transport. EIS measurements were carried out at V OC under one-sun illumination. Fig. 6 shows the Nyquist plots based on several electrolytes under the optimal conditions. Three semicircles were observed in the impedance spectra. The first one at high-frequency fitted to a charge-transfer resistance (R ct1) at the electrolyte–Pt interface. The second arc at midfrequency was governed by the charge-transfer process (R ct2) at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte. The third semicircle at low-frequency fitted to the Warburg diffusion process of I−/I3 − (R diff) in the electrolyte. The Ohmic serial resistance, R s, is associated with the series resistance of the electrolytes and electric contacts in the DSSCs [27]. The fit results were presented in Table 4 . Comparing with ILs electrolytes, all R were lower for the carbon dots/ILs-based electrolytes, indicating that the introduction of carbon dots reduced the charge transfer resistance. Moreover, all R of carbon dots/[bmim][PF6]-based blend electrolyte were smaller than R of carbon dots/[bmim][BF4]-based electrolyte. From the photovoltaic performances of DSSCs, it was also observed that η of [bmim][PF6] based electrolyte was higher. Decreasing resistance of carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] corresponds to the increase of electron mobility, which leads to the improvement of the cell efficiencies. [bmim]I electrolyte exhibited the highest R.

The ionic conductivity (σ = t/AR b, ‘t’ is the thickness of the film and ‘A’ is the area of the sample) was calculated from the bulk electrolyte resistance (R b) value obtained from the complex impedance diagram in Fig. 6. The conductivity values of electrodes at room temperature were also listed in Table 4. The increase in σ can explain the increase in mobility of I−/I3 − species in the electrolyte.

The decay test was carried out in order to further examine the photovoltaic performance of carbon dots/ILs-based blend DSSCs. I–V measurements were monitored repeatedly at different time intervals and the results were shown in Table 5 . Within the test time, V OC decreased constantly, while J SC increased firstly and then decreased. After placing for 10min at ambient temperature, η of the cells reached up to a maximum value. The first increased behavior was understandable because the fully contact between the electrolyte and TiO2 photoanode (or N719) need a durative soakage period (about 10min) to achieve high J SC and η [28]. With prolonged time, irreversible intercalated Li ions could occur and gradually accumulate in the photoelectrode film, leading to the decrease in J SC and η [29].

Carbon dots/ionic liquids were prepared using ionic liquid-assisted electrochemical exfoliation. UV–vis spectrum of carbon dots/ILs showed that a comparatively low absorption in the visible region was observed. It was suggested that carbon dots/ILs were suitable for use as electrolyte in DSSCs. The addition of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide and LiI/I2 enhanced the performance of DSSCs. EIS analyses showed that the electron transports were enhanced in the carbon dot/ILs-based blend electrolytes. η were 2.71% and 2.41% for carbon dots/[bmim][PF6] and carbon dots/[bmim][BF4]-based blend electrolytes, respectively. A 82% enhancement in η was obtained by introduction of carbon dots into [bmim][PF6] comparing with [bmim][PF6]. The cells prepared with carbon dots/ILs-based blend electrolytes exhibited improved stability under continuous illumination in room temperature without any further sealing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Special Prophase Program for Key Basic Research of the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (973 Program: 2010CB234606), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51102087, 11105047), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2010CDB04706).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A low-cost, high-efficiency solar cell based on dye-sensitized colloidal TiO2 films

Science

Applied Physics Letters

Journal of Physical Chemistry B

Journal of the American Chemical Society

Electrochimica Acta

Chemistry of Materials

Journal of the American Chemical Society

Advanced Functional Materials

ACS Nano

Progress in Photovoltaics: Research and Applications

Chemistry of Materials
